structure.description: Hierarchical-tree body supports drag-to-reparent as primary affordance for `replace .parentId` / `replace .folderId` intents. No 'Move to…' modal needed for the hierarchical case — drag target onto folder = commit. Modal stays available for keyboard-driven path. Effect is generated declaratively from intent + tree structure, not coded per-domain.

rationale.hypothesis: Replace-FK intents on tree-shaped entities are the prototypical drag-target operation. Forcing a modal ('Choose new parent…') breaks spatial reasoning — the user already knows where they want it because they can see the tree. Drag commits the spatial decision directly. The modal remains as keyboard fallback, not the primary path.

evidence.description: File explorer: drag note onto folder = move; modal exists but is secondary
Page tree supports drag-to-reparent; 'Move to…' is secondary

counterexample.description: Issues are flat per-project; no hierarchy to drag into
Org-tree changes are formally approved; drag would bypass invariants

shouldMatch.reason: Page.parentPageId is self-ref FK with replace intent — drag-reparent fits

shouldNotMatch.reason: Position has no parent; flat list
Reparenting is approval-gated; UX must surface the approval, not hide it behind a drag
